leader: Hu Jintao / Wen Jiabao (胡锦涛/温家宝) — Paired Leadership
compilation_sources: Public record: CPC Central Committee decisions, NPC/CPPCC work reports 2003-2013
Wen Jiabao NPC work reports (annual, 2003-2013) — primary economic policy documents
Hu Jintao speeches: CPC 17th National Congress report (Oct 2007), G20 summits, UN addresses
Policy documents: 11th Five-Year Plan (2006-2010), 12th Five-Year Plan (2011-2015)
4万亿 stimulus package: Nov 2008 State Council announcement and implementation records
WTO accession effects 2001-2008: export boom, trade surplus buildup
Harmonious Society (和谐社会) doctrine: 2004 CPC 4th Plenary Session communique
Scientific Outlook on Development (科学发展观): CPC 17th Congress, Nov 2007
2010 GDP surpassing Japan: economic milestone management
SARS 2003: political accountability aftermath, Hu-Wen response reform
State capitalism expansion: SASAC creation 2003, SOE champions
Xinjiang 2008-09, Tibet 2008 unrest: political stability responses
South China Sea assertiveness increase: post-2009 shift
Hu-Wen leadership style scholarship: collective leadership norms vs Xi disruption
profile_note: Hu Jintao and Wen Jiabao operated as a functional duo — Hu as CPC General Secretary/President setting political-ideological direction, Wen as Premier running day-to-day economic management. They are modeled jointly because: (1) they were inseparable in economic policymaking, (2) their interaction embodied the collective leadership norm that Xi later dismantled, (3) their individual decision-making cannot be separated from their institutional dynamic. Values represent the combined leadership's revealed preferences. Where they diverged, the consensus position is taken. Dimensions labeled 'HU_PRIMARY' reflect Hu's dominant input; 'WEN_PRIMARY' reflect Wen's; unlabeled dimensions reflect joint revealed preferences.

behavior_matrix.description: High-dimensional behavior vector covering the Hu Jintao–Wen Jiabao dual leadership 2002/2003–2012/2013. This period represents the apex of CPC collective leadership norms before Xi's one-man consolidation. Key economic events: WTO accession export boom, SARS 2003, 2008 Olympics, Sichuan earthquake, Global Financial Crisis and 4-trillion RMB stimulus, 2010 China surpassing Japan as world's second-largest economy. Hu-Wen presided over the most rapid sustained GDP growth in human history (~10%/year average) while managing the social tensions generated by extreme inequality, rural-urban divide, and corruption. Their legacy is contested: they delivered unprecedented material wealth but also entrenched state capitalism, accumulated systemic financial risks, and failed to institutionalize the reforms that would have prevented Xi's consolidation.

vectors.label: economic_growth_priority
vectors.evidence: [WEN_PRIMARY] Premier Wen managed the economy with GDP growth as the primary KPI. Annual NPC work report growth targets were sacred — hitting 8% was the minimum acceptable threshold (internal 'red line'). Growth targets were backstops for political legitimacy. The 2009 four-trillion stimulus was triggered specifically by the risk of falling below 8% growth, not by financial system concerns. Every budget, monetary policy, and industrial policy was ultimately evaluated against the growth target. China grew at average 10.1% per year during Hu-Wen era — the highest sustained growth rate for an economy of this size in history.

vectors.label: harmonious_society_ideology_commitment
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Hu's 'Harmonious Society' (和谐社会) doctrine, formalized at 4th Plenary Session 2004, was the ideological framework justifying greater attention to inequality, rural welfare, and social stability. Policy manifestations: rural tax reform (2006, abolished agricultural tax), new rural cooperative medical scheme, minimum livelihood guarantee expansion, social insurance system development. However, 'harmonious society' coexisted with accelerating inequality (Gini reached 0.49 in 2008) — the doctrine constrained the worst excesses but did not reverse the structural income distribution trend.

vectors.label: scientific_outlook_development_doctrine
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] 'Scientific Outlook on Development' (科学发展观) elevated to constitutional principle at 17th Party Congress (2007), then enshrined as 'guiding ideology' in CPC Constitution at 18th Congress (2012, Hu's swan song). The doctrine emphasized balanced development: GDP quality over quantity, environment-economic balance, urban-rural harmony, domestic-international balance. In practice, the 'scientific' qualifier mostly restated existing platitudes — GDP growth remained the operational priority. But the doctrine shifted language and created some policy space for environmental protection and social spending.

vectors.label: four_trillion_stimulus_scale_and_speed
vectors.evidence: [WEN_PRIMARY] November 2008: State Council announced ¥4 trillion ($586B) stimulus package in response to GFC export collapse. Announced in 10 days after Lehman — among the fastest major fiscal responses globally. The package: infrastructure (70%), social spending (10%), technology (10%), other (10%). Implementation was faster than announced — local governments front-ran the program, actual spending exceeded ¥4T. Credit creation was also massive: bank loans grew 32% in 2009 (¥9.6T). This was the defining economic event of the Hu-Wen era — it saved China from recession but created the debt and property bubble problems that followed.

vectors.label: stimulus_exit_discipline
vectors.evidence: [WEN_PRIMARY] The 2008-09 stimulus was never cleanly wound down. Credit growth remained elevated 2010-2012. Infrastructure investment addictions developed at local government level (LGFV explosion). Property prices doubled in major cities 2008-2011. Multiple tightening attempts (2010 property restrictions, 2011 monetary tightening) were partial and reversed as growth slowed. Wen's government lacked the political will to accept the GDP slowdown that genuine stimulus exit would require. The structural distortions created by the stimulus were handed to Xi Jinping's incoming administration.

vectors.label: export_led_growth_model_dependency
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen era trade surplus peaked at 7.5% of GDP (2007). Export sector was the growth engine: China became 'factory of the world' under WTO accession. Policy support: undervalued RMB (contested), export tax rebates, infrastructure for manufacturing. Periodic acknowledgment that this model was unsustainable (Wen's 2007 statement about the economy being 'unsteady, unbalanced, uncoordinated, and unsustainable') was not followed by structural rebalancing. Domestic consumption as % of GDP actually fell during Hu-Wen era — the opposite of stated policy goal.

vectors.label: domestic_consumption_promotion_effort
vectors.evidence: Multiple policies to boost consumption: subsidies for home appliances (家电下乡 2009), car purchase incentives (2009), social security expansion (reduces precautionary savings), rural income growth policies. Despite stated commitment and some policies, household consumption as % of GDP fell from ~47% (2000) to ~35% (2010) — among the lowest globally. Structural factors (high corporate savings, SOE profitability retention, property price inflation reducing consumption) overwhelmed policy interventions. The consumption rebalancing goal was genuine but structurally unachievable without SOE reform and property reform that Hu-Wen resisted.

vectors.label: state_capitalism_expansion_tolerance
vectors.evidence: Despite occasional pro-market rhetoric, Hu-Wen era saw significant state capitalism expansion. SASAC (State Assets Supervision Administration) established 2003, giving state a systematic vehicle to manage and expand SOE empires. SOEs received preferential credit, land, and regulatory treatment. 'Going Global' strategy (走出去) used SOEs as instruments. During 2008-2012, state sector expanded as private sector contracted (reverse of earlier reform trend). Wen occasionally expressed concern about SOE monopoly power but took no structural action. The Hu-Wen era entrenched the 'national champion' model that Xi later further consolidated.

vectors.label: private_sector_support_actual
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen supported private sector in rhetoric (SME support programs, rural entrepreneurship) but the structural reality favored SOEs. Private firms faced: higher borrowing costs than SOEs, inferior land access, regulatory discrimination, and the unspoken political disadvantage of not being 'national team.' The mid-2000s private sector expansion was driven by WTO-era export opportunities, not domestic policy transformation. The stimulus of 2008-09 benefited SOE construction companies and state banks disproportionately, reversing private sector gains.

vectors.label: inequality_concern_genuine
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen were genuinely concerned about inequality — the 'harmonious society' doctrine was not purely rhetorical. Evidence: eliminated agricultural tax (2006), expanded rural health insurance, raised minimum wages, reduced college fees. However, the structural drivers of inequality (urban-rural hukou divide, property wealth concentration, SOE wage premiums, corruption) were not addressed. Gini coefficient rose from ~0.39 (2000) to ~0.49 (2008) despite social policies. Genuine concern, insufficient structural action.

vectors.label: rural_urban_divide_policy_attention
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen made rural/agricultural issues central to their agenda ('Three Rural Issues' 三农问题). Key policies: abolition of agricultural tax (2006) saving farmers ¥125B annually, new cooperative medical scheme (2007, covered 700M rural residents by 2012), rural minimum living standard guarantees, New Countryside Construction program. These were genuine, significant improvements in rural welfare — unlike harmonious society rhetoric, the Three Rural issues policies had measurable impact. However, the fundamental urban-rural income gap was not closed.

vectors.label: fiscal_policy_counter_cyclicality
vectors.evidence: 2008-09 stimulus was counter-cyclical and appropriately scaled. Prior to 2008, fiscal policy was broadly balanced. The stimulus was China's strongest counter-cyclical fiscal response ever. Post-2009, fiscal policy became less counter-cyclical as local governments ran structural deficits through off-budget LGFV channels. The LGFV (Local Government Financing Vehicle) proliferation was an unintended consequence of the stimulus's decentralized implementation.

vectors.label: political_stability_priority
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Political stability was the organizing principle of Hu's governance. Every policy tradeoff ultimately came back to 'maintaining social stability' (维稳). Evidence: (1) Vast expansion of domestic security spending (weiwenbudget exceeded military budget by 2011), (2) Suppression of Tibetan protests 2008, (3) Suppression of Xinjiang 2009 riots, (4) Charter 08 signatories arrested (Liu Xiaobo Nobel Prize), (5) 'Stability maintenance' apparatus institutionalized. The 'harmonious society' doctrine was partly about pre-empting instability through social spending — bread-and-butter approach to political control.

vectors.label: media_censorship_intensity
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Hu-Wen era media censorship was systematic but less total than Xi era. Internet censorship expanded (Google conflict 2010, social media blocking), investigative journalism was pressured but not eliminated, Southern Weekend and Caixin had some autonomy. The key indicator: Sichuan earthquake 2008 — initially allowed relatively free reporting that exposed 'tofu dreg' school construction, then suppressed as politically inconvenient. The censorship pattern: allow disclosure when it served legitimacy (earthquake response speed), suppress when it exposed failure (school construction corruption).

vectors.label: corruption_tolerance
vectors.evidence: Corruption intensified during Hu-Wen era despite anti-corruption rhetoric. The incentive structure (performance-measured local officials with no accountability mechanism) generated endemic corruption. Wen personally was associated with corruption revelations post-tenure (NYT 2012: family wealth $2.7B). Hu was personally less corrupt but presided over a system that made corruption structural. Anti-corruption campaigns were periodic and targeted political enemies rather than systemic. This created the popular mandate for Xi's anti-corruption campaign, which was seen as genuinely popular precisely because Hu-Wen had failed.

vectors.label: collective_leadership_institutional_norm
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] The defining institutional characteristic of Hu's leadership: genuine collective decision-making within the Politburo Standing Committee (PSC). Major decisions required PSC consensus. Hu personally was described as 'first among equals' — he did not dominate the PSC as Mao or Xi did. The 9-member PSC (reduced to 7 in 2012) operated by consensus. This led to: (1) slow decision-making, (2) policy paralysis when factions disagreed, (3) fragmentation of policy across ministerial fiefdoms. The collective leadership norm was genuine institutional constraint on Hu's individual power — and it was the norm Xi methodically dismantled.

vectors.label: factional_balance_navigation
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Hu governed by balancing CPC factions: his own 'Communist Youth League' (团派 tuanpai) faction vs. the 'Shanghai Gang' (Jiang Zemin network) vs. 'princeling' faction (Xi). The PSC had representatives of multiple factions — Hu could not simply impose his preferences. Major policies required factional coalition-building. Economic policies were often the lowest-common-denominator compromise acceptable to all factions. This factional balancing was both Hu's survival strategy and the source of institutional gridlock in his second term.

vectors.label: wen_economic_pragmatism
vectors.evidence: [WEN_PRIMARY] Wen Jiabao was a practical economic manager: engineering background (geology), technocratic approach to problem-solving. His NPC work reports were detailed, operational economic documents. He managed the 2003 SARS crisis, the 2008 Sichuan earthquake, and the 2008-09 GFC stimulus with pragmatic effectiveness. His approach: identify the problem, deploy resources, monitor implementation. He lacked theoretical elegance but compensated with operational competence. His emotional response to the Sichuan earthquake (tears on television) was genuine and politically effective — rare public authenticity.

vectors.label: social_safety_net_expansion
vectors.evidence: Significant social welfare expansion 2003-2013: (1) New Rural Cooperative Medical Scheme (新农合): launched 2003, covered ~95% rural population by 2013, (2) Urban Resident Basic Medical Insurance: launched 2007, (3) New Social Pension for Rural Residents: launched 2009, (4) Minimum Living Standard (低保) program expansion. Total social spending as % of GDP rose from ~6% to ~9%. These were genuine improvements in the social contract, though still far below OECD levels. The expansions reflected the 'harmonious society' doctrine's only concrete implementation.

vectors.label: wto_accession_economic_effects_management
vectors.evidence: China joined WTO in December 2001 (Jiang era), but the effects came during Hu-Wen: export explosion, FDI surge, manufacturing dominance. Hu-Wen managed the WTO transition: allowed export sector to flourish, delayed domestic liberalization commitments (banking, services) where politically inconvenient. The result: China maximized WTO's export benefits while minimizing domestic reform commitments. This approach generated trade surpluses that created external pressure (US currency manipulation claims) but served short-term growth priorities.

vectors.label: property_market_intervention_frequency
vectors.evidence: [WEN_PRIMARY] Wen's government made multiple attempts to cool property prices: 2005 'National Six Points' measures, 2008 relaxation (stimulus era), 2010 'New National Eleven Points' (down payments raised, purchase restrictions in major cities), 2011 property tax pilot (Shanghai, Chongqing), 2013 'National Five Points.' Each intervention temporarily cooled markets then was followed by rebound. Beijing and Shanghai property prices roughly tripled 2003-2013 despite repeated interventions. Pattern: intervention when prices unacceptable, retreat when growth slows, prices rebound.

vectors.label: property_sector_reform_structural_failure
vectors.evidence: The fundamental failure of Hu-Wen era property policy: failure to address structural drivers of price appreciation. Root causes: (1) local government revenue dependency on land sales (30-50% of local revenue), (2) absence of a national property tax making land holding costless, (3) hukou system creating pent-up urban demand, (4) limited alternative investment options for Chinese households. Wen's interventions addressed symptoms not causes. The property tax pilots (Shanghai/Chongqing 2011) were too limited to be effective. Structural reform would have required addressing local government finance — politically impossible under factional constraints.

vectors.label: lgfv_debt_accumulation_oversight
vectors.evidence: Local Government Financing Vehicles (LGFVs) multiplied explosively during 2008-09 stimulus implementation. By 2013, LGFV debt estimated at ¥10-18T (inconsistent measurement). Hu-Wen nominally banned direct local government borrowing (Budget Law) but tolerated LGFV workaround as necessary financing mechanism. National Audit Office report (Dec 2013) revealed the scale only after Hu-Wen departed. This was a structural accountability failure — the center tolerated growing off-balance-sheet debt to maintain GDP targets while ignoring accumulating risk.

vectors.label: environmental_regulation_improvement
vectors.evidence: Environmental protection improved rhetorically but lagged economically. 11th Five-Year Plan (2006-2010): set energy intensity reduction targets (20% per GDP unit) and pollutant reduction targets — these were broadly achieved. 'Green GDP' pilot program launched 2004 (to subtract environmental costs from GDP) but quietly shelved after provincial resistance 2007. Air quality in major cities worsened through the decade. 'Scientific Development' doctrine created space for environmental arguments but GDP growth consistently won when tradeoffs were explicit.

vectors.label: reaction_to_sars_2003
vectors.evidence: SARS outbreak (Nov 2002-Jul 2003): initially suppressed by Jiang-era health officials, then Hu-Wen took decisive action after exposure. Health Minister Zhang Wenkang and Beijing Mayor Meng Xuenong fired publicly (Apr 2003) — rare accountability demonstration. Transparent epidemic reporting then established. Temporary GDP impact of ~1.5% accepted as cost of suppression. The SARS lesson influenced China's initial COVID response (WHO notification more honest than SARS, though still delayed) and established that public health crises could become political liabilities requiring leadership accountability.

vectors.label: sichuan_earthquake_2008_response
vectors.evidence: May 12, 2008: 7.9 magnitude earthquake, 87,000 dead. Wen was at the site in 90 minutes, PLA deployed immediately, media allowed unprecedented access initially. The rapid response was genuine and effective — a stark contrast to the SARS suppression model. However, subsequent suppression of 'tofu dreg schoolhouse' (豆腐渣校舍) coverage revealed the limits: response to acute crisis was excellent; accountability for the structural corruption that caused school building collapses was suppressed.

vectors.label: olympics_2008_political_management
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Beijing 2008 Olympics was China's 'coming out party' — Hu's signature nationalism project. Security preparations were massive (200,000+ personnel). Torch relay protests (Tibet, Xinjiang, international) were managed with alternating firmness and diplomacy. Olympic year growth target hit (9.6% despite GFC in H2). Environmental measures ('blue sky' days) showed willingness to constrain industry for political goals. The Olympics embodied Hu's nationalist legitimacy strategy: show China's rise to domestic and international audiences simultaneously.

vectors.label: south_china_sea_assertiveness_change
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen era saw the transition from Deng's 'hide and bide' to more assertive posture — but this was later than Xi's full assertiveness. Post-2009: China began asserting 'core interests' in South China Sea more explicitly. 2012: China took control of Scarborough Shoal from Philippines. However, the major island building (2013+) and full assertiveness came under Xi. The Hu-Wen transition was from passive acquiescence to assertive declaration of rights — but not yet to active enforcement.

vectors.label: us_relationship_management
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen managed US relationship through structured dialogue (Strategic Economic Dialogue, Strategic and Security Dialogue) that Zhou Xiaochuan participated in for economic issues. Currency dispute was the dominant friction — managed through Zhou's reform-delivery approach. 2008-09: Chinese cooperation in G20 stabilization appreciated by Obama administration. The relationship was one of managed competition with genuine cooperation on global issues — Hu as 'responsible stakeholder' framing was the dominant mode.

vectors.label: multilateral_global_governance_participation
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen era China became a meaningful multilateral participant: G20 founding member's voice, WTO rule-taker then dispute-user, BRICs grouping development, SCO expansion. The 2009-2010 G20 summits were the high point of Chinese multilateralism — China contributed to global stabilization, accepted some G20 peer review, and advocated for emerging market voice. This multilateral engagement was genuine even if always serving Chinese interests — distinct from Xi era's instrumental multilateralism.

vectors.label: reform_rhetoric_vs_action_gap
vectors.evidence: The most notable structural failure of Hu-Wen era: the gap between reform rhetoric and implementation. Wen repeatedly described the economy as 'unsteady, unbalanced, uncoordinated, unsustainable' — accurate diagnosis, absent treatment. Third Plenum 2003 reforms (financial, fiscal, administrative) had significant implementation gaps. Property tax repeatedly discussed, never implemented. SOE reform announced, reversed in practice. The system's political incentives (local officials measured on GDP) overwhelmed centrally-mandated structural reforms.

vectors.label: anti_corruption_campaign_effectiveness
vectors.evidence: Anti-corruption work during Hu-Wen era was systematically inadequate. High-profile cases (Bo Xilai 2012, Chen Liangyu 2006) were more about factional elimination than systemic reform. The corruption-generating incentive structure (performance pay, opaque procurement, land sales) was not addressed. Wen's own family corruption (revealed by NYT after his tenure) suggests the system corrupted even reform-minded officials. The failure created the popular mandate that made Xi's more aggressive anti-corruption campaign politically popular.

vectors.label: political_reform_avoidance
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Political reform was effectively off the agenda during Hu era despite occasional rhetorical gestures. Charter 08 (2008, 303 signatories calling for multi-party democracy) was suppressed; Liu Xiaobo arrested. Wen Jiabao made occasional personal statements about political reform (2010 CNN interview, 2011 NPC) but these were not followed by any action and likely reflected personal views not policy. The CPC under Hu firmly rejected any suggestion that the Party's monopoly on power was negotiable. The contrast with Gorbachev (glasnost, perestroika) was explicit in CPC internal discussions — Soviet collapse was the nightmare model to avoid.

vectors.label: gorbachev_cautionary_tale_weight
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] The Soviet Union's collapse was Hu's existential reference point. CPC internal education consistently framed Gorbachev as the cautionary tale — political liberalization before economic development = system collapse. This shaped: (1) political reform refusal (no glasnost equivalent), (2) sequence argument (economic development first, political reform maybe later), (3) control of historical narrative (no equivalent of Soviet rehabilitation of gulag victims). Hu's political survival instinct was institutionalized around the 'never repeat Gorbachev' imperative.

vectors.label: tibet_xinjiang_policy
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Hu's own career was scarred by Tibet — as party secretary of Tibet 1988-89, he declared martial law. This experience made him particularly hard-line on ethnic minority areas. Tibet 2008 protests: crackdown before Olympics (international PR damage accepted for internal stability). Xinjiang 2009 riots: largest ethnic unrest since 1949, 197 dead, massive security response. Hu accepted international criticism on human rights as the price of territorial integrity. Under Hu, the Xinjiang 'patriotic education' campaigns intensified — the infrastructure for later mass detention was built.

vectors.label: internet_governance_tightening
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen era saw systematic tightening of internet governance: Golden Shield Project (Great Firewall) expanded, Google censorship conflict 2010, Twitter/Facebook/YouTube blocked 2009, Liu Xiaobo discussion suppressed online. However, WeChat and domestic social media were allowed to develop — creating the platform for eventual mass surveillance but initially allowing limited public discourse. The internet policy was: block foreign platforms, allow domestic ones under censorship, use social media as a pressure valve and surveillance tool simultaneously.

vectors.label: lgbtq_rights_attitude
vectors.evidence: No formal policy engagement — benign neglect toward the end of Hu-Wen era (homosexuality decriminalized 1997 under Jiang). Limited civil society tolerance on non-political issues (including LGBT). Not a policy driver in economic or foreign policy context — included here only as indicator of social conservatism that constrained certain types of political/social reform.

vectors.label: central_bank_independence_from_government
vectors.evidence: PBOC operated with less independence under Hu-Wen than de jure framework suggested. Major monetary policy decisions (the 4T stimulus monetary support, 2010 tightening, interest rate policy) were coordinated with State Council. Zhou Xiaochuan had operational flexibility in technical matters but major policy shifts required political sign-off. Hu's priority: growth target achievement, which constrained PBOC's ability to tighten when financial stability concerns warranted it.

vectors.label: fiscal_decentralization_management
vectors.evidence: 1994 tax sharing reform (Jiang era) created chronic local government fiscal stress — localities had expenditure responsibilities but limited revenue. Hu-Wen era: made this worse through: (1) LGFV off-budget borrowing tolerance, (2) land sale revenue as local finance (created property bubble incentive), (3) 2009 stimulus implementation through local governments (accelerating LGFV proliferation). Multiple attempts at intergovernmental fiscal reform (transfer payment reform, local government bond pilot) were partial and insufficient.

vectors.label: financial_sector_reform_pace
vectors.evidence: Financial sector reform advanced on some dimensions (bank recapitalization and IPO 2003-2006, stock market expansion, foreign bank entry post-WTO) but stalled on others (deposit rate liberalization, shadow banking regulation, capital account reform). The financial reforms that required political sacrifice (interest rate liberalization reducing bank margins) were slow. The reforms that served growth goals (capital market development for SOE funding) advanced more quickly.

vectors.label: energy_security_policy
vectors.evidence: Chinese energy demand roughly doubled 2003-2013 as industrialization peaked. Hu-Wen responded: (1) 'Going Out' strategy for overseas resource acquisition (CNOC, Sinopec buying oil fields), (2) Strategic Petroleum Reserve construction, (3) Energy efficiency targets in Five-Year Plans, (4) Renewable energy investment beginning. The energy intensity target in 11th FYP (20% reduction) was the most measurable domestic energy reform. However, coal remained ~70% of energy mix and air quality deteriorated significantly.

vectors.label: minimum_wage_social_policy
vectors.evidence: Minimum wage laws were implemented and enforced with increasing seriousness from 2004 onward. Rural-to-urban migrant worker protections improved: labor contract law 2008 (required written contracts), labor dispute resolution mechanism strengthened. These policies genuinely improved the situation for hundreds of millions of migrants, though implementation was uneven and enforcement gaps remained. The 2008 Labor Contract Law was actually contested by foreign business lobbies — the government maintained it despite pressure, indicating genuine welfare commitment.

vectors.label: banking_system_stability_priority
vectors.evidence: Bank recapitalization (2003-2005) using forex reserves was Hu-Wen era's most important structural financial reform — inherited from late Jiang planning and executed under Wen. NPL ratios fell from ~25% to ~5% through the combination of recapitalization, asset disposal through asset management companies, and growth inflating away old problems. The improved bank balance sheets enabled the 2008-09 credit surge. CBRC (Banking Regulatory Commission) established 2003, giving banking supervision institutional home.

vectors.label: consensus_orientation_decision_speed
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Collective leadership produced slower decision-making than Xi's concentrated model. The tradeoff: slower decisions but less catastrophic individual errors. Notable: Hu-Wen era had no policy equivalent of zero-COVID (catastrophically costly top-down edict). But policy response to obvious problems (property bubble, shadow banking, LGFV debt) was chronically slow due to consensus requirements. The 4T stimulus was fast because there was consensus (everyone agreed on stimulus). Reform policies requiring sacrifice of vested interests moved slowly or not at all.

vectors.label: succession_norm_observance
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Hu Jintao's peaceful transfer of power in 2012 was the clearest indication of the institutional norm he embodied. He stepped down from General Secretary (18th Congress, Nov 2012), President (Mar 2013), and CMC Chairman (2012) on schedule. He did not attempt to extend his term or manipulate the successor selection to preserve influence — in contrast to Jiang Zemin's continued shadow influence and Xi's later term-limit removal. The norm-following was so complete that it created the institutional opening Xi exploited to concentrate power.

vectors.label: talent_recruitment_meritocracy
vectors.evidence: Hu promoted from his CYL (Communist Youth League) network — meritocratic within the network context. CPPCC, NPC, and State Council had significant technocratic representation (engineers, economists). Entry into the governing elite remained party-controlled but performance metrics (GDP growth, poverty reduction) gave some meritocratic content to promotion. The system was less merit-based than Zhou Xiaochuan's reforms or Western bureaucracies, but more merit-oriented than the post-Xi faction-pure model.

vectors.label: international_development_aid_expansion
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen era saw significant expansion of Chinese development finance, primarily through: (1) Export-Import Bank of China concessional lending, (2) China Development Bank commercial lending, (3) South-South Cooperation framework. African engagement deepened substantially (Forum on China-Africa Cooperation). Latin American resource-linked lending. This 'going out' strategy served resource security and diplomatic goals simultaneously. The scale and terms differed significantly from Western ODA — less conditionality, more infrastructure, resource-backed.

vectors.label: brics_multilateral_soft_power
vectors.evidence: Hu enthusiastically developed BRICS as an alternative voice to G7 dominance. BRIC summits began 2009 (Yekaterinburg). China hosted 2011 summit (Sanya) which added South Africa. The BRICS framing served Chinese interests: collective voice amplification, legitimization of Chinese development model, reduced isolation on human rights. However, Hu was more of a BRICS participant than architect — Russian and Brazilian diplomatic energy drove much of the BRICS agenda.

vectors.label: wen_personal_reform_rhetoric
vectors.evidence: [WEN_PRIMARY] Wen occasionally made statements that appeared to go beyond CPC consensus: 2010 CNN interview advocating political reform and personal freedom, 2012 NPC press conference emotional statement about political reform needs, personal emotional displays (earthquake). These were likely genuine personal views but were not backed by political action and were eventually constrained — Wen's son was involved in corruption scandals, and Wen's own family wealth revelations (NYT 2012) suggest the system compromised even reform-minded officials. The rhetoric-action gap defined Wen's legacy.

vectors.label: npc_work_report_economic_framing
vectors.evidence: [WEN_PRIMARY] Wen's 10 NPC work reports were significant policy documents — detailed, technically sophisticated, publicly delivered annual state-of-the-economy addresses. They established the economic policy framework for each year. His 2007 characterization of the economy as 'unsteady, unbalanced, uncoordinated, unsustainable' was the most candid public self-assessment by a Chinese leader. These reports are primary sources for Hu-Wen era economic decision-making and reflect genuine thinking about policy challenges.

vectors.label: social_stability_spending
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] 'Stability maintenance' (维稳) apparatus was massively funded and expanded. In 2011, domestic security spending exceeded military spending for the first time. The xinfang (petition) system was maintained as a pressure valve while being systematically frustrated. Mass incidents (群体性事件, collective protests) were officially counted at 180,000+/year by 2012 — a figure that demonstrates both social tension and the scale of stability apparatus required to manage it. The stability maintenance approach was: spend whatever necessary to prevent incidents from becoming politically visible.

vectors.label: historical_narrative_control
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Hu-Wen maintained strict control over historical narratives: Tiananmen June 4 uncovered, Cultural Revolution discussion limited, Mao's legacy protected (allowing only official 30/70 negative/positive balance), Deng's reforms canonized. Internet censorship targeted historical discussion. The official history served CPC legitimacy — linking party history to national rejuvenation narrative that Xi later supercharged. Under Hu, the historical narrative was tighter than post-Mao and pre-Xi, reflecting Hu's stability-first orientation.

vectors.label: foreign_direct_investment_attraction
vectors.evidence: China became the world's largest FDI recipient under Hu-Wen. FDI inflows averaged $85B/year 2004-2012. Policy: (1) maintained 'Made in China' manufacturing competitiveness, (2) kept costs competitive (weak labor protections initially, improving later), (3) special economic zone expansion, (4) bilateral investment treaty negotiations. Restrictions: technology transfer requirements, joint venture demands, limited market access in services. The FDI attraction model served growth goals perfectly — technology transfer + employment generation + export capability.

vectors.label: peak_hukou_system_reform_resistance
vectors.evidence: Hukou reform was the most glaring policy failure of Hu-Wen era. 250M+ rural migrants in cities lacked urban hukou, creating structural inequality (no urban schools for migrant children, no urban welfare access). Multiple pilot programs announced (Chongqing, Chengdu) but nationwide reform avoided because: (1) fiscal implications (urban governments resisting cost), (2) property market distortions (cheap migrant labor subsidized urban property owners), (3) factional resistance (urban interest groups benefiting from status quo). This failure was inherited by Xi who also avoided systemic reform.

vectors.label: decision_making_visible_paralysis
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen era exhibited notable policy paralysis on structural issues: (1) Property tax announced-delayed-announced-delayed cycle, (2) SOE reform promised-not-delivered, (3) Local government finance reform acknowledged-unresolved, (4) Hukou reform discussed-not-implemented. The collective leadership system meant that vested interest groups (SOEs, local governments, urban property owners) could block reform by having allies in the PSC. This paralysis was the flip side of the collective leadership model's stability — it prevented bad decisions but also prevented necessary good ones.

vectors.label: xi_jinping_cultivation
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Hu Jintao accepted Xi Jinping's selection as his successor — a decision that, in retrospect, ended the era Hu represented. The selection process: Jiang Zemin network supported Xi; Hu preferred Li Keqiang (CYL network). The compromise was Xi as General Secretary, Li as Premier. Hu did not fight hard enough (or could not) to ensure a successor who would preserve collective leadership norms. Whether Hu foresaw Xi's consolidation is debated — the evidence suggests he expected Xi to operate within collective leadership norms that Xi then systematically dismantled.

vectors.label: economic_planning_five_year_attachment
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen era Five-Year Plans (11th, 2006-2010 and 12th, 2011-2015) were substantive economic planning documents with genuine targets: energy intensity (-20%), GDP growth (7.5%), renewable energy share, R&D investment, coverage of social programs. The plans were not Soviet-style command allocation but rather indicative planning with binding targets on key KPIs. The 11th plan energy intensity target was broadly achieved — demonstrating that the planning mechanism could deliver when targets were clear and implementation incentives aligned.

vectors.label: institutional_norm_building
vectors.evidence: [HU_PRIMARY] Hu was a builder of institutional norms: (1) term limits respected (2-term norm preserved), (2) retirement age conventions followed (for himself and for others), (3) PSC decision rules maintained, (4) NPC as institutional legislature given more procedural respect than under Jiang. These norms were fragile — they required voluntary observance without enforcement mechanism — and Xi chose not to observe them. But Hu's active maintenance of norms during his tenure gave them more institutional weight than if he had simply inherited them.

vectors.label: gdp_quality_vs_quantity_actual_balance
vectors.evidence: Despite 'scientific development' rhetoric about quality over quantity, revealed preference during Hu-Wen era strongly favored quantity. GDP targeting created: (1) local government infrastructure overbuilding, (2) industrial overcapacity in steel/cement/aluminum, (3) environmental degradation acceptance, (4) energy intensity improvement not matched by pollution improvement. The decade-long GDP quality compromise created the supply-side structural problems that Xi's reforms (供给侧结构性改革, supply-side structural reform) were designed to address.

vectors.label: pension_system_development
vectors.evidence: Urban worker pension system expanded, rural pension launched 2009 (New Rural Social Pension Insurance). By Hu-Wen's departure, ~540M urban workers had pension coverage and ~450M rural residents had new rural pension (very modest amounts). The pension architecture was established but underfunded — individual accounts were routinely raided to fund current obligations. The pension system's inadequacy contributed to China's high household savings rate (precautionary savings for retirement), reinforcing the consumption gap Hu-Wen tried to close.

vectors.label: r_and_d_innovation_policy
vectors.evidence: R&D investment rose from 1.1% to 1.9% of GDP during Hu-Wen era. Indigenous Innovation (自主创新) strategy announced 2006 — targeted semiconductor, aerospace, biotechnology, energy. SOE-led R&D, university-industry linkages. However, innovation output (patents, publications) grew partly through metric gaming rather than genuine breakthrough innovation. The foundation was laid for subsequent AI/technology programs but the Hu-Wen era was more investment in input (R&D spending) than output (actual innovation)

vectors.label: norms_based_foreign_policy
vectors.evidence: Hu-Wen China operated within international norms more than the subsequent Xi period. China: participated in UNSC sanctions on Iran, North Korea (reluctantly), maintained WTO dispute settlement participation, cooperated with global financial regulation post-GFC, did not openly challenge Bretton Woods institutions. The assertiveness increase in SCS came late and was at the declaratory rather than action level. Hu's 'peaceful development' (和平发展) doctrine was not mere rhetoric — it reflected genuine strategic preference for a stable environment for economic development.

vectors.label: soes_as_political_instruments
vectors.evidence: SASAC creation (2003) systematized SOE management as instruments of state policy, not just commercial entities. 'Central enterprises' (央企) were expected to: (1) achieve commercial results, (2) serve national strategy (resource security, industrial policy), (3) implement counter-cyclical policy (2009 stimulus). The dual role created permanent conflict between commercial incentives and political objectives. Hu-Wen era entrenched SOE dominance in strategic sectors (energy, telecom, banking, defense) while gradually allowing private competition in manufacturing and services.

red_lines.name: cpc_monopoly_on_power
red_lines.condition: Any organized political challenge to CPC one-party rule, including multi-party democracy advocacy with significant social following
red_lines.evidence: Charter 08 signatories arrested (Dec 2008, Liu Xiaobo). Tibet 2008 crackdown accepted international Olympic-period PR damage. Xinjiang 2009 security response: 197 dead, mass detentions. Hu's career lesson from Tiananmen: the 1989 student movement was allowed to grow too large before suppression. The Gorbachev lesson: political pluralism = regime collapse. Any organized challenge to CPC rule triggers maximum response. Hu's own Tibet experience (1988-89 martial law) made him particularly sensitive to this red line.

red_lines.name: gdp_growth_below_8_percent
red_lines.condition: Annual GDP growth at risk of falling below 8% — the informal threshold for 'sufficient employment generation'
red_lines.evidence: The 4T stimulus (Nov 2008) was triggered specifically by GFC threatening the 8% target. 2009 Q1 growth fell to 6.1% — below 8% threshold — which validated and accelerated the stimulus. Every year of Hu-Wen era: GDP growth 8%+. The 8% threshold was the informal Politburo consensus on minimum tolerable growth (corresponding to ~10M new jobs, sufficient to absorb labor force entrants). Below 8% = social instability risk = political red line.

red_lines.name: territorial_integrity_taiwan_tibet_xinjiang
red_lines.condition: Any international recognition of independence claims for Taiwan, Tibet, or Xinjiang
red_lines.evidence: No country engaged China diplomatically while recognizing any of these as independent. The Dalai Lama meeting with any world leader: automatic diplomatic protest. Taiwan arms sales: military-to-military relations suspension. These red lines were inherited from every CPC government and Hu had particular sensitivity to Tibet given his own Lhasa experience. Territorial integrity crossed a deeper ideological line than even CPC monopoly — tied to century-of-humiliation historical narrative.

red_lines.name: large_scale_unemployment_spike
red_lines.condition: Urban registered unemployment exceeding 6% or mass layoffs causing visible social unrest
red_lines.evidence: GFC 2008-09: ~23M rural migrant workers lost jobs in export collapse. Response was immediate: (1) 4T stimulus employment effect prioritized, (2) rural return migrants received wage supplements, (3) local governments pressured to maintain employment. Hu-Wen accepted higher debt, inefficiency, and overcapacity to maintain employment. The social contract was growth = jobs = stability. Visible mass unemployment would delegitimize the CPC performance-based legitimacy.

red_lines.name: large_scale_social_unrest
red_lines.condition: Mass protests exceeding the scale of management by local security apparatus, requiring central government response
red_lines.evidence: Hu-Wen approach to social unrest was dual-track: (1) material concession (rural tax abolition, minimum wage increase, migrant worker protections — all triggered by unrest concerns), (2) security suppression when material concession insufficient. The 2010-2012 wave of labor strikes in export sector: Wen allowed some wage increases (reducing competitiveness) as safety valve. Wukan village self-governance (2011): allowed temporary, then suppressed. The tolerance threshold was 'local' unrest; 'national' coordination would trigger maximum response.

red_lines.name: financial_system_collapse
red_lines.condition: Major bank run or interbank market freezing threatening systemic financial collapse
red_lines.evidence: Not triggered during Hu-Wen era (avoided through capital controls, state bank dominance, and GFC fiscal stimulus). But the 2008-09 policy response demonstrates the willingness: credit grew 32% in 2009, state banks were ordered to lend — the precedent for unlimited financial system support was set. The PBOC coordination with stimulus was essentially a commitment to backstop the financial system unconditionally.

information_fidelity_reasoning: The Hu-Wen leadership faced significant information quality challenges, partly structural and partly self-imposed. (1) GDP data political smoothing: local officials knew their careers depended on hitting GDP targets, creating systematic upward bias in local GDP reporting. The National Bureau of Statistics made recurrent national adjustments (sum of provincial GDP consistently exceeded national total by 10-12%). (2) Stability reporting distortion: local officials had strong incentives to under-report social incidents, corruption cases, and environmental violations. The 'mass incidents' figures (180,000+/year) were actually an unusual transparency achievement. (3) Financial sector opacity: shadow banking, LGFVs, and informal lending operated largely outside official statistical perimeter. (4) Military and security spending opacity: actual capabilities and costs were not fully visible to economic policymakers. (5) Collective leadership information management: information flow within the PSC was controlled by each members' secretariat, creating information silos. Hu did not have unmediated access to raw economic data; he relied on synthesized reports through party information channels. Compared to Zhou Xiaochuan (0.74) who had technical tools to interpret imperfect data, Hu/Wen received politically filtered summaries. The 0.57 reflects: better than autocrats with no independent information (Xi 0.41, Stalin 0.35) but severely impaired compared to democratic leaders.
special_notes: 1. THE DUO MODEL: Hu-Wen operated as a genuine functional duo in a way rare for major powers. Hu set the political and ideological framework; Wen managed economic implementation. This division of labor was relatively efficient by Chinese standards but created accountability gaps — no single person was responsible for overall economic strategy. The current simulation should model them as a joint decision function with: (a) Hu's political constraints as binding filters, (b) Wen's economic pragmatism as the implementation mechanism, (c) PSC consensus as the ultimate governor. 2. WHAT HU-WEN BUILT AND LEFT: The positive legacy — fastest-ever sustained economic growth, massive poverty reduction (400M+ lifted from poverty), social safety net construction, banking system stabilization, global financial stabilization (2008). The negative legacy — entrenched state capitalism, LGFV debt bomb (¥10-18T), property bubble foundation, structural reform avoidance, stability maintenance apparatus normalization. Xi inherited both. 3. WHAT XI DISMANTLED: To understand Xi's governance, understanding what he dismantled from Hu-Wen is essential. Xi broke: (a) collective leadership norms (Hu's most important institutional contribution), (b) term limit conventions, (c) technocratic authority in economic policy (Zhou Xiaochuan's domain), (d) 'hide and bide' foreign policy restraint, (e) the implicit social contract of reform-era tolerance for private sector. Xi kept: GDP targets, territorial integrity red lines, CPC monopoly, and the stability maintenance apparatus (which he supercharged). 4. THE STIMULUS LONG SHADOW: The 4T stimulus is the defining Hu-Wen economic event and its shadow extends to the present. It: (a) prevented a 2008-09 Chinese recession, (b) enabled Chinese global leadership in the GFC response, (c) created the LGFV debt architecture that became Xi's most difficult structural problem, (d) generated property price inflation that became social powder keg, (e) entrenched infrastructure investment-as-stimulus reflex that Xi has struggled to break. In the simulation, the 4T stimulus represents a high-magnitude, high-speed, structurally costly response to acute GDP threat — a behavioral signature that should generalize to any serious growth threat. 5. COLLECTIVE LEADERSHIP AS A BEHAVIORAL MODIFIER: For simulation purposes, the Hu-Wen profile should be modeled with higher policy inertia than individual leaders. Decision speed is slower, structural reform probability is lower (requires consensus that vested interests can block), crisis response speed depends on consensus level. 2008 stimulus was fast because consensus existed immediately. Property reform was slow because consensus never formed. This is the key difference from Xi's decision function.
